title: Let's talk about The Everrain (and Sleeping Gods, Pirate Borg, and Godshard)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=h1qNF-YaHX8

Daniel (@dungeondive) is careful to stress this is not a review of The Everrain — he never finished a single game, and doubts he will. After five or six days with it set up (it does fit a small 3x4 solo table), he simply found he wasn't having fun, and explains why, with constant comparison to the game it most resembles: Sleeping Gods.

The Everrain shares the premise he loves — captain a ship, explore a vast ocean, find islands, fight monsters, live out narrative encounters — but for every choice Sleeping Gods made toward snappy, turn-by-turn fun, The Everrain seems to make the opposite. Everything is stretched out, apparently to simulate the vastness of the sea: movement crawls under a navigation-token economy where issued orders lock until later turns; the story system is bafflingly convoluted, dispersing across many turns what Sleeping Gods resolves in one; the island-exploration deck is a thin ten cards; and fiddly per-crew traits demand constant cross-checking. The rulebook is genuinely poor — disorganised enough that even the Universal Head summary couldn't rescue it.

He's clear it isn't a badly designed game, just not for him, and it's a shame because the theme scratches all his itches. He name-checks Pirate Borg and Godshard as livelier alternatives, and is selling his copy.

----

Do you prefer a game that stretches mechanics to *simulate* a vast world, or one that condenses the same adventure into snappy, always-doing-something turns?
